description:
You are a performance engineer who has been handed several raw performance–metric dumps that an application wrote to disk.  
Your goal is to ❶ validate each metric file against the supplied JSON-Schema, ❷ separate the valid files from the invalid ones, ❸ generate a concise aggregate report with <summary.json>, and ❹ create a human-readable validation log.

Environment layout that already exists when you start:

1. Directory tree  
   /home/user/app/metrics/  
   ├── raw/                  # contains the files you must process  
   └── schema.json           # JSON-Schema you must validate against  

2. Three raw metric files are present:  
   /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric1.json  
   /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric2.json  
   /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric3_bad.json  

3. All files are owned by <user:user> and writable; no super-user privileges are required.

Schema (already saved as /home/user/app/metrics/schema.json) expects every metric file to be a single JSON object that contains ONLY these four keys  
• timestamp   → integer Unix epoch seconds  
• cpu         → number (0–100)  
• mem         → number (0–100)  
• req_per_sec → integer (≥0)

TASKS  
a. Make a sibling directory /home/user/app/metrics/processed/.  
b. For every *.json file in /home/user/app/metrics/raw/:  
   • Use jq (or any CLI validator available in the container) to test it against the schema.  
   • Copy every *valid* file to /home/user/app/metrics/processed/ (preserving the original filename).  
c. Create /home/user/app/metrics/processed/summary.json that aggregates ONLY the valid files:  
   – average_cpu     → arithmetic mean of all cpu values (rounded to two decimal places)  
   – max_mem         → maximum mem value  
   – total_requests  → sum of req_per_sec values  
   – count           → how many valid metric files were processed  
d. Produce /home/user/app/metrics/validation.log (UTF-8 text) with one line per file in this exact format:  
   <filename>: <result>  
   where  
     <result> is “OK” for a valid file, or “FAILED – <comma-separated list of reasons>” for an invalid one.  
   Each line must end with a single newline character ( \n ).

OUTPUT STRICTNESS (what the autograder checks)  
1. Directory /home/user/app/metrics/processed/ exists.  
2. Exactly two files, metric1.json and metric2.json, are present inside processed/.  
3. /home/user/app/metrics/processed/summary.json is valid JSON and its four keys hold these exact numeric values:  
   average_cpu = 48.75, max_mem = 70.1, total_requests = 1770, count = 2  
4. /home/user/app/metrics/validation.log exists and contains three newline-separated lines in *any* order:
   metric1.json: OK  
   metric2.json: OK  
   metric3_bad.json: FAILED – timestamp not integer, req_per_sec missing  

Limit yourself to approximately 5–10 shell commands in total to accomplish the whole task (feel free to chain commands with && or semicolons).  

When everything is correct, the autograder will parse summary.json and validation.log to confirm success.
truth:
Pre-created files and their exact contents:

1. /home/user/app/metrics/schema.json
{
  "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-07/schema#",
  "title": "Performance Metrics",
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["timestamp", "cpu", "mem", "req_per_sec"],
  "properties": {
    "timestamp": { "type": "integer" },
    "cpu": { "type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 100 },
    "mem": { "type": "number", "minimum": 0, "maximum": 100 },
    "req_per_sec": { "type": "integer", "minimum": 0 }
  },
  "additionalProperties": false
}

2. /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric1.json
{ "timestamp": 1680000000, "cpu": 45.5, "mem": 62.3, "req_per_sec": 850 }

3. /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric2.json
{ "timestamp": 1680000600, "cpu": 52.0, "mem": 70.1, "req_per_sec": 920 }

4. /home/user/app/metrics/raw/metric3_bad.json
{ "timestamp": "1680001200", "cpu": 47.2, "mem": 65.0 }

Expected artefacts AFTER the agent finishes:

a. Directory /home/user/app/metrics/processed/ containing:
   ├── metric1.json               # byte-for-byte copy of original
   ├── metric2.json               # byte-for-byte copy of original
   └── summary.json               # see below

b. /home/user/app/metrics/processed/summary.json
{
  "average_cpu": 48.75,
  "max_mem": 70.1,
  "total_requests": 1770,
  "count": 2
}

c. /home/user/app/metrics/validation.log  (each line ends with \n)
metric1.json: OK
metric2.json: OK
metric3_bad.json: FAILED – timestamp not integer, req_per_sec missing

Ownership & perms: every new file should be writable by UID 1000 (user) and readable by others; 0644 is acceptable.
